entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: it is seen in the case of the word as well

entities.name: vaktṛ
entities.cite: one who possesses the whole and must nonetheless produce it serially

entities.name: buddhivisaya
entities.cite: the object of a single cognition

entities.name: tritaya
entities.cite: the thing is first apprehended in sequence, then held as the object of a single sequenceless cognition, then externalized in sequence again

axioms.name: utpadyate
axioms.cite: realized through the staged procedure of articulation

axioms.name: vivartate
axioms.cite: the word's permanent oscillation between unity and sequence

axioms.name: kriya
axioms.cite: painting is deliberate craft, and it models the *speaker's* situation

denials.name: natural
denials.cite: Fire, reflection and egg are natural processes, but painting is deliberate craft

claims.name: ekacaitanya
claims.cite: though the object of a single cognition

claims.name: utpadyate
claims.cite: it is seen in the case of the word as well

rejected_readings.label: mutual_exclusivity
rejected_readings.rendering: The word is either unified in the speaker's mind or sequential in articulation, but not both simultaneously.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's central teaching is that the word is permanently dual-natured: unified (ekacaitanya) in cognition yet realized through the three-stage procedure (tritaya utpadyate). The commentary emphasizes 'the word's permanent oscillation between unity and sequence', demonstrating they coexist. Denying this simultaneity violates the vivartate axiom.

rejected_readings.label: natural_process
rejected_readings.rendering: The word is produced spontaneously like natural processes (fire, reflection, eggs), without the speaker's deliberate crafting.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's choice of painting (not natural phenomena) emphasizes deliberate craft and speaker agency. The commentary explicitly contrasts: 'Fire, reflection and egg are natural processes, but painting is deliberate craft, and it models the *speaker's* situation'. Denying the speaker's intentional kriya (agency) contradicts both the kriya axiom and the denial that sabda is natural.

notes: The contract captures verse 1.52's teaching that the word (sabda) is simultaneously unified in the speaker's cognition (ekacaitanya) while being realized through a three-stage procedure of articulation (tritaya utpadyate). The verse emphasizes deliberate speaker agency (kriya) through the painting simile, distinguishing linguistic production from natural processes. The traditional reading interprets the tritaya as a recurrent cycle: sequential apprehension, unified cognition, sequential externalization—each utterance re-enacting this oscillation (vivartate).
